coredata.dc:title: CuCo2O4 supported graphene quantum dots as a new and promising catalyst for methanol oxidation reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Madadi, Masoumeh
Salarizadeh, Parisa
Rohani Moghadam, Masoud
Bazmandegan-Shamili, Alireza
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the design of an active electrocatalyst with a unitary structure and high electrocatalytic performance as an efficient and low-cost electrode material has received enormous attention in improving the performance of energy and storage devices. In this study, flower-like CuCo2O4 nanorods are synthesized through the hydrothermal method followed by calcination. Then, CuCo2O4 is hybridized with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared by the pyrolysis method. The structure and morphology of the synthesized electrocatalysts are analyzed by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction methods. The electrochemical performance of the prepared materials supported on a glassy carbon electrode is investigated by cyclic voltammetry at different methanol concentrations and various scan rates. Also, chronoamperometry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy techniques are performed for the evaluation of stability and conductivity. The GQD/CuCo2O4 exhibits a good electrochemical surface area of 19.25 cm2 in 1 M KOH and 4 M methanol. Also, GQD/CuCo2O4 shows a higher current density for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) than CuCo2O4. The superior electrochemical performance of the GQD/CuCo2O4 can be attributed to the strengthening of the CuCo2O4 nanoparticle structure by a large electrochemically active surface associated with nanoporous morphology in the presence of GQDs. Besides, GQDs enhance the catalyst's performance by increasing electrical conductivity and specific surface area.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuCo2O4 nanorods
Graphene quantum dots
Electrocatalyst
Methanol oxidation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CuCo2O4/GCE and GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE
Electrochemical properties of catalysts for methanol oxidation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Department of Chemistry and High-Temperature Fuel Cell Research Department for supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of synthesis of CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4.
(a) UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra (inset) of the GQDs, (b) Raman spectra of CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4, (c) XRD patterns of CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4, and (d) FTIR spectra of CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4.

SEM images of the CuCo2O4 nanorods (a, b) and GQD/CuCo2O4 nanocomposites (c); TEM images of CuCo2O4 (d), GQD/CuCo2O4 nanocomposite (e, f), and GQDs (g); EDX mapping of Cu, Co, and O elements in CuCo2O4 (h) and Cu, Co, O, and C GQD/CuCo2O4 (i).
CV curves of CuCo2O4 (a) and GQD/CuCo2O4 (b) in a potential range of 0.1–0.3 V at different scan rates. Plots of current density versus scan rates of CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4 (c).
CV curves of GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE, CuCo2O4/GCE, and bare GCE catalysts recorded in (a) 1 M KOH (b) 1 M KOH with 4 M methanol at scan rate of 20 mV s−1 within a potential window of 0–0.9 V (vs. Ag/AgCl).

(a) CV curves of the CuCo2O4/GCE and (b) CV curves of the GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE, in 1 M NaOH solution and different concentrations of methanol, potential scan rate was 20 mV s−1 within a potential window of 0–0.9 V (vs. Ag/AgCl).

CV curves of CuCo2O4/GCE (a) and GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE (b) recorded at different scan rates. Linear plots related to peak current density versus square root of scan rate for (c) CuCo2O4/GCE and (d) GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE.
(a) Chronoamperometry results of prepared samples for 500 s and (b) Nyquist plots recorded at a potential of 0.75 V for GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE and CuCo2O4/GCE in 1 M KOH and 4 M methanol solution.
Comparison of the catalytic activity for methanol oxidation.

head.ce:title: CuCo2O4 supported graphene quantum dots as a new and promising catalyst for methanol oxidation reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Vali-e-Asr University of Rafsanjan, Rafsanjan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Vali-e-Asr University of Rafsanjan, Rafsanjan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: High-Temperature Fuel Cell Research Department, Vali-e-Asr University of Rafsanjan, Rafsanjan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: High-Temperature Fuel Cell Research Department, Vali-e-Asr University of Rafsanjan, Rafsanjan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the design of an active electrocatalyst with a unitary structure and high electrocatalytic performance as an efficient and low-cost electrode material has received enormous attention in improving the performance of energy and storage devices. In this study, flower-like CuCo2O4 nanorods are synthesized through the hydrothermal method followed by calcination. Then, CuCo2O4 is hybridized with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared by the pyrolysis method. The structure and morphology of the synthesized electrocatalysts are analyzed by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction methods. The electrochemical performance of the prepared materials supported on a glassy carbon electrode is investigated by cyclic voltammetry at different methanol concentrations and various scan rates. Also, chronoamperometry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy techniques are performed for the evaluation of stability and conductivity. The GQD/CuCo2O4 exhibits a good electrochemical surface area of 19.25 cm2 in 1 M KOH and 4 M methanol. Also, GQD/CuCo2O4 shows a higher current density for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) than CuCo2O4. The superior electrochemical performance of the GQD/CuCo2O4 can be attributed to the strengthening of the CuCo2O4 nanoparticle structure by a large electrochemically active surface associated with nanoporous morphology in the presence of GQDs. Besides, GQDs enhance the catalyst's performance by increasing electrical conductivity and specific surface area.

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel cells that can run directly on fuels such as methanol and are known as direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) due to their low weight, low pollution, low operating temperature, and volume relative to other power sources have some benefits like facile utilization, easy storage, and transportation. The slow kinetics of the methanol electrooxidation reaction is one of the main problems in DMFCs facing researchers. Up to now, numerous attempts have been made to solve this problem of alkaline solutions as electrolytes, and the development of efficient electrocatalysts to increase methanol electrooxidation can solve this challenge to a large extent [1,2]. Using alkaline solutions as electrolytes has plenty of advantages such as high efficiency, the stability of different kinds of electrode materials on its surface, a significant performance of both anodic and cathodic reactions, and negligible poisoning of the electrode surface. It is also apparent that the structure of the catalyst material is an essential factor for methanol oxidation [3].

Recently, transition metal oxides such as binary metal have obtained increasing attention due to their superior conductivity and electrochemical activity compared to monometallic oxides have been highly investigated as electrode modifiers materials because they possess numerous oxidation states that can enhance electrochemical redox reactions [3,4]. In the previous reports, bimetallic oxides, such as NiCo2O4 [5], ZnCo2O4 [6], CuCo2O4 [7], MoCo2O4 [8], MgCo2O4 [9], FeCo2O4 [10], CoWO4-CoMn2O4 [11], CoMn2O4 [12], CuFe2O4 [13], have been considered for electrochemical applications. In these compounds, the synergistic effects between metal cations that create a lot of electrochemical activity and stability. Especially, CuCo2O4, the most common element of cobaltite, has drawn considerable attention. Therefore, the CuCo2O4 catalyst was chosen as a suitable catalyst for MOR, a key component in DMFCs in terms of activity and stability [14]. The CuCo2O4 based electrode materials, compared with their counterparts, have a lower density, larger surface area, and low cost and can be promising catalytic materials. A wide range of preparation techniques has been reported for producing oxide nanoparticles, including sol–gel [15], photochemical, chemical deposition, spray-pyrolysis, electrocoating, and hydrothermal methods. In order to enhance the electrochemical activity of CuCo2O4, much attention has been allocated to easy synthesis, morphology engineering, and chemical structural optimization. The hydrothermal method is a simple and efficient method supported by various precursors and solvents under very high pressure and temperature. This method, over other types of crystal growth, includes creating crystalline phases unstable at the melting point. Also, hydrothermal can grow materials with high vapor pressure near their melting points. The method is also particularly suitable for growing large, good-quality crystals while maintaining control over their composition [16].

It’s be noted that for using metal oxides as electrocatalyst, their efficiency should be improved by using several different strategies, like morphology controlling, conducting substrate incorporation, hetero-structuring, and creating more active surface. For this mean, carbonic materials are generally used as support catalysts, such as carbon, carbon nanotube, and carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

GQDs are a type of graphene-based nanomaterials that are the result of the chemical oxidation process of carbon materials, so they can be considered very small derivatives of graphene oxide. GQDs consist of one or more graphene layers. These nanomaterials can exist in two functional and non-functional forms so that they are functionalized through chemical oxidation with carboxyl (–COOHOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups [17]. Their electronic structure depends on the crystallographic orientation of their edges, for example, zigzag edge GQDs with a diameter of 7–8 nm exhibit metallic behavior. Today, GQDs have attracted much attention in nanotechnology materials due to their necessary properties, including large surface area (2630 m2/g), high thermal conductivity, low toxicity, high fluorescence activity, good stability, high optical and catalytic properties, and excellent solubility [18]. In addition, the surface of GQDs contains many carboxylic and hydroxyl groups, which give them good water solubility and control the synthesis of the incompact small dendritic core–shell nanostructure by the better surface grafting using π–π* conjugation and surface groups, thus improving the electrocatalytic performance of the compounds [19]. GQDs can present a fast track of activating water and accelerating the transformation of chemisorbed CO to CO2 [20]. Recently, GQDs have been noticed for sensing [21], catalysis [22], and energy [23] applications owing to excellent electricalconductivityand high stability. Besides, GQDs prevent agglomeration of metal or metal oxide nanoparticles and provide high active sites. For example, Li et. al prepared Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb, which exhibited efficient electrocatalytic performance for MOR due to channel-rich structure of catalyst [24]. Mahato et. al synthesized S,N-GQD/TiO2 supported with carbon for oxygen evolution reaction. The catalyst showed outstandingcatalytic activity with good stability [22].

In this work, CuCo2O4 nanorods and a specific nanocomposite of graphene quantum dot and CuCo2O4 with porous morphology (GQD/CuCo2O4) were prepared. CuO2O4 was synthesized by the hydrothermal method. In order to prepare GQD/CuO2O4 nanocomposite, a certain amount of the synthesized CuO2O4 was dispersed in the GQD and subjected to ultrasound. The electrochemical properties of catalysts for MOR were investigated. The results demonstrated enhanced electrocatalytic activity of GQD/CuCo2O4 for MOR in an alkaline medium. Based on the conductivity of GQDs, the presence of functional groups containing oxygen on the surface, strong interaction between CuCo2O4 and GQDs, it creates an effective composite. So, GQD/CuCo2O4 nanoparticles show more electroactivity compared to CuCO2O4.

Copper nitrate(Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), Cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), Urea, potassium chloride (KCl), and potassium hydroxide (KOH), Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH), methanol (MeOH), Citric acid (CA), Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were obtained from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany). An Autolab system potentiostat–galvanostat (Autolab, model PGSTAT 302N, The Netherlands) using a NOVA (2.1) software was exerted on electrochemical experiments at room temperature. A platinum wire and a silver-silver chloride (Ag/AgCl, saturated KCl) electrode were utilized as auxiliary and referenced electrodes, respectively. The EIS with an FRA 32 impedance analysis module was exerted for electrode surface investigation.

Morphology, structure, and composition of synthesized CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4 were characterized using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (FESEM, ZEISS, Germany - Sigma VP model), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (EM10C-100KV device, Zeiss, Germany), Raman spectroscopy (XploRA Plus model, HORIBA Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet ISIO, USA), X-ray diffraction (XRD) (XPert Pro device, Panalytical Inc) and Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) (BElSORP Mini model from Microtrac Bel Corp) method. Electrochemical measurements were performed at a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Co., Shanghai, China).

CuCo2O4 nanorods were synthesized via facile hydrothermal method using mixing 1 mmol of Cu(NO3)2, 2 mmol of Co(NO3)2, and 0.4 g of urea in 50 ml of deionized water. The reaction mixture was magnetically stirred in an open container for 30 min, and then it was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and placed at 160 °C for 18 h. After that, the autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature, and the product was washed with distilled water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, the resulting powder was calcined at 400 °C for 2 h to obtain CuCo2O4.

The general approach for synthesizing GQDs is bottom-up, which is easy method. For the preparation of GQDs, citric acid (2 g) was turned into a liquid by heating at 200 °C. After 30 min of heating, the color of melted citric acid changed from colorless to orange, which indicated the formation of GQDs. Finally, the obtained orange liquid was neutralized (pH = 7) using NaOH solution (10 mg/ml), and the prepared GQDs solution was maintained at 4 °C [18]. For the preparation of GQD/CuCo2O4, 0.01 g of CuCo2O4 was dispersed in 1 ml GQD under stirring for 1 h at 60 °C. The prepared solution composite was used for electrode preparation. The schematic of the synthesis procedure is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CuCo2O4/GCE and GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: GCE was polished with 0.05 mm alumina slurry on a microfiber cloth to obtain a mirror surface and placed in deionized water under sonication for 2 min. Then, it was conditioned in 0.1 M sulfuric acid by 15 successive cyclic voltammetry cycles (from −1 to 1.5 V) at a scan rate of 0.05 V s−1. To prepare the CuCo2O4/GCE, an amount of 10 mg of CuCo2O4 was dispersed in 1 ml of isopropyl alcohol and 10 µl of Nafion for 30 min by ultrasonic method to obtain a homogenous solution. Then, 6 µl of the suspension was placed on GCE and dried at room temperature. For GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE preparation, 1 µl of Nafion solution was added to 1 ml of GQD/CuCo2O4 solution, and 6 µl of the resultant was poured on GCE and dried at room temperature.

All the electrochemical studies were conducted at room temperature using various methods such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a three-electrode system. This system consisted of GCE modified electrode as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl (Sat.) as the reference electrode, and Pt as the counter electrode. Experiments were carried out in an alkaline electrolyte in the absence and presence of methanol. CV was taken at the scan rate of 20 mV s−1 within the potential window of 0–0.9 V. Chronoamperometry test was carried out at a constant potential of 0.75 V for 500 s, and EIS was taken in the frequency range of 1 Hz to 100 kHz at an applied potential of 0.75 V. The double layer capacitance (Cdl) was determined from CV curves recorded in different scan rates ranging from 20 to 100 mV s−1 in 1 M KOH solution.

The synthesized GQD was characterized by UV–Vis, and photoluminescence as shown in Fig. 2a. UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs shows a strong absorption below 300 nm and a broad peak in 356 nm, which represent π-π* and n-π* transitions of CC and CO, respectively (Fig. 2a) [25].

The photoluminescence spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths of 280 – 380 nm are shown in inset of Fig. 2a. The emission wavelength of GQDs is nearly excitation-dependent and shifts from 392 nm to 670 nm when the excitation wavelength increases from 280 to 380 nm. These shifts reflect the sp2 clusters in GQDs should be in different sizes that it is compatible with its TEM image.

Raman analysis was used to determine the structural properties ofthe synthesized materials. TheRaman spectraof CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4 samples are shown in Fig. 2b. The peaks at 186, 484, 620, and 691 cm−1 are attributed to F2g, Eg, F2g, and A1g modes of the CuCo2O4 spinel. No additional bonds are observed, indicating purity of CuCo2O4 spinel. In the Raman spectrum of GQD/CuCo2O4, vibration modes of F2g, Eg, F2g, and A1g are seen at 298, 408, 472, 677, and 724 cm−1. Besides, vibration modes of GQDs are observed at 1373 and 1577 cm−1, which attributed to D band and G bands of GQDs.

The XRD pattern of the cubic spinel phase CuCo2O4 electrocatalyst is shown in Fig. 2c. The peaks at 2θ = 18.9, 31.27, 36.89, 38.6, 55.38, 59.37, 65.29, and 77.3◦ are attributed to (111), (220), (311), (222), (422), (511), (440), and (533) crystal planes, respectively, which confirm the cubic spinel phase of CuCo2O4, and it is in good agreement with JCPDs No. 01–1155 [26]. Also, GQD/CuCo2O4 show diffraction peaks at 2θ = 18.9, 31.27, 36.89, 38.6, 55.38, 59.37, 65.29, and 77.3◦, which correspond to (111), (220), (311), (222), (422), (511), (440), and (533) crystal planes of CuCo2O4, respectively. No peak is seen for GQDs due to their low amount and low diffraction intensity of GQD [27]. The GQDs/CuCo2O4 nanocomposites showed lower full-width at half maximum (FWHM) than CuCo2O4, which means a decrease in crystallite size [28]. The molecular structure of the synthesized compounds was investigated by FT-IR, as shown in Fig. 2d. The broad peaks at around 3450 cm−1 are attributed to the –OH stretching band, and the peak centered at 1619 cm−1 indicates the presence of the bending modes related to absorbed water molecules. The vibrations corresponding to the carbonate group were observed at 1384 cm−1 (str, vCO3) and 1018 cm−1 (sh, v CO) [29]. Besides, two adsorption bands indicated at 562 cm−1 and 658 cm−1 correspond to the vibration of (Co-O and Cu-O) ions bond inside the lattices of spinel structure, and it corresponded to the site of octahedral and tetrahedral [30]. These bands differed in frequency due to the length of the oxygen-metal bond in octahedral and tetrahedral sites. For this reason, a high frequency of stretching vibration appeared at the tetrahedral site, while the low frequency was related to the vibration of the octahedral.

The FTIR spectrum of prepared GQD/CuCo2O4 is shown in Fig. 2d. A broad peak at 3450 cm−1 is corresponded to the OH stretching vibration and the peak at 1281.29 cm−1 is assigned to OH bending vibration in C-OH. Moreover, this functional group improves the hydrophilicity and stability of GQDs in aqueous systems. The peak at 1581 cm−1 corresponds to CC stretching vibrations and the peak at 1385 cm−1 corresponds to the symmetric stretching vibration of CO in carboxylic functional groups [31].

The surface morphology and structural features were investigated by FE-SEM and TEM techniques, as displayed in Fig. 3 . The SEM images of prepared CuCo2O4 nanorods and GQD/CuCo2O4 nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3. Comparing the SEM images of CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4, expresses that the addition of GQD changes the morphology of CuCo2O4. In Fig. 3a and b, CuCo2O4 structure formed from nanorods with 52–89 nm in thickness, and nanorods are connected to obtain flower-like morphology. Whereas the GQD/CuCo2O4 nanocomposites consist of uneven surfaces with particles-accumulative, which result in wider special surface and active sites for the progress of electrochemical reactions (Fig. 3c). As expected, the TEM image of CuCo2O4 nanorods showed in Fig. 3d. In GQD/CuCo2O4 nanocomposite TEM image, CuCo2O4 nanoparticles are distributed on GQD surface (Figs. 3e and f). Spherical morphology of GQDs with the particle size of lower than 15 nm is observed in Fig. 3g. The EDX mapping images of CuCo2O4 (Fig. 3h) and GQD/CuCo2O4 (Fig. 3i) indicate the homogenous distribution of Cu, Co, O, and C elements and show the presence of GQDs in the GQD/CuCo2O4 nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of catalysts for methanol oxidation
ce:section.ce:para:
One of the methods often used to determine the electrochemical surface area is an electrochemical measurement of the double-layer capacitance. Therefore, the double layer capacitance (Cdl) was determined from CV curves in different scan rates in the non-faradic region for GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE and CuCo2O4/GCE (Fig. 4 a and b) [32]. Cdl was obtained from the slope of the plot of current density versus scan rate (Fig. 4c and d). The values of Cdl were calculated at 0.77 and 0.37 mF/cm2 for GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE and CuCo2O4/GCE, respectively. From Cdl, it is possible to calculate the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) by the following equation 1:
E C S A =
C d l
C s

For the determination of the ECSA, specific capacitance (Cs) is required. This value explains the capacitance of an ideal flat surface of the catalyst. The Cs values for different catalysts vary between 0.022 and 0.130 mF/cm2 in alkaline solutions, which is about 0.04 mF cm−2 in KOH solution. Using the above-mentioned equation, the ECSA for GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE and CuCo2O4/GCE was obtained to be 19.25 and 9.25, respectively.

The electrocatalytic activity of GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE for methanol electrooxidation was analyzed by CV technique in 1 M KOH electrolyte with and without 4 M methanol. Also, the CuCo2O4/GCE and bare GCE were compared under the same conditions in Fig. 5 a, b. The CV curves of GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE, CuCo2O4/GCE, and bare GCE were recorded at a scan rate of 20 mV s-1 within the potential window of 0–0.9 V in 1 M KOH (Fig. 5a). The GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE shows a significant increase in current density compared to CuCo2O4/GCE and bare GCE confirming its high electrochemical activity. Furthermore, Fig. 5b shows the CV curves of GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE, CuCo2O4/GCE, and bare GCE in 1 M KOH with 4 M methanol solution at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. Adding methanol to an aqueous KOH solution significantly increases current densities for all tested catalysts. The GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE and CuCo2O4/GCE exhibit current densities of 176 and 25 mA cm−2, respectively. The observed current density of the GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE catalyst is about 8-fold higher than CuCo2O4/GCE. These observations are related to the high conductivity of GQD and its high specific surface area and inherent activity of CuCo2O4, making a synergic effect of improving the catalyst's performance. It is no doubt that GQD plays an essential role in the improvement of the catalytic activity of CuCo2O4 towards the electrooxidation of methanol.

In the next step, the various concentration from 0.5 to 5 M methanol was added to 1 M KOH, and the electrochemical data was recorded for GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE and CuCo2O4/GCE. Fig. 6 a and 6b show that the anodic current density increases with increased methanol concentration at these two electrodes. GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE shows higher current density than CuCo2O4/GCE due to the synergistic effect of CuCo2O4 and GQD that simultaneously enhances the conductivity and surface area of the catalyst.

Fig. 7 a and b display the effect of scan rate on the methanol oxidation in 4 M methanol on CuCo2O4/GCE and GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE, respectively. The potential grows by increasing the scan rate from 20 to 100 mV s-1, and the current oxidation increases linearly. The relation between the oxidation current versus the square root of the scan rate was brought in Fig. 7c and 7d. The current oxidation increases linearly with the square root of the scan rate ((υ) ½). These observations illustrate that the methanol oxidation reaction is under mass transfer control [33].

Stability is another important parameter for evaluating the electrocatalytic properties of catalysts. For this purpose, chronoamperometry was measured in 1 M KOH and 4 M methanol solution at a potential of 0.5 V for 500 s. As shown in Fig. 8 a, in all of the electrocatalysts' chronoamperometry curves, current density decreased at the initial stage, which can be attributed to the catalysts poisoning via the cumulation of intermediate products on the CuCo2O4 active sites [34]. GQD/CuCo2O4 catalysts show higher stability than that CuCo2O4. The current density of GQD/CuCo2O4 after 500 s is higher than that of CuCo2O4.

Nyquist plot related to CuCo2O4/GCE and GQD/ CuCo2O4/GCE is shown in Fig. 8b. It consists of a quarter circle and a Warburg line. The fitted equivalent circuit (Inset of Fig. 8b) includes charge transfer resistance (Rct), Warburg, and double-layer capacitance (C). Rct for CuCo2O4/GCE and GQD/CuCo2O4/GCE obtained 1070 and 850 Ω, respectively. Thus, GQD/CuCo2O4 shows higher electrical conductivity, faster electron transfer, and an ion diffusion rate than CuCo2O4. The performance of the synthesized catalysts with other similar works, compared in Table 1 , represents the excellent performance of the prepared catalysts compared to others.

In summary, we successfully synthesized CuCo2O4 and GQD/CuCo2O4 as an efficient electrocatalysts. The introduction of GQDs changed the morphology of CuCo2O4 and formed a unique nanostructure with high electrochemical activity. The addition of GQD leads to excellent electrochemical performance, improved conductivity, and enhanced ECSA. The maximum current density for GQD/CuCo2O4 obtained 176 mA cm−2 in 0.7 V at the presence of 4 M methanol, while CuCo2O4 indicated 25 mA cm−2 at the same condition. According to these results, it can be said that GQDs have great promise in electrochemical applications and it is helpful for further improvement in electrodes for electrochemical reactions.

Masoumeh Madadi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Parisa Salarizadeh: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Masoud Rohani Moghadam: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration. Alireza Bazmandegan-Shamili: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Department of Chemistry and High-Temperature Fuel Cell Research Department for supporting this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient direct-methanol fuel cell based on graphene quantum dots/multi-walled carbon nanotubes composite
Electroanalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Güneştekin, B., et al., Efficient Direct‐Methanol Fuel Cell Based on Graphene Quantum Dots/Multi‐Walled Carbon Nanotubes Composite. Electroanalysis, 2020. 32.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Active direct methanol fuel cell: An overview
Int. J. Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alias, M.S., et al., Active direct methanol fuel cell: An overview. International Journal of Hydrogen Energy, 2020. 45(38): p. 19620-19641.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of structure, composition, and carbon support properties on the electrocatalytic activity of Pt-Ni-graphene nanocatalysts for the methanol oxidation
Appl Catal B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, Y., et al., Effects of structure, composition, and carbon support properties on the electrocatalytic activity of Pt-Ni-graphene nanocatalysts for the methanol oxidation. Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 2012. 111-112: p. 208-217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mn–Ni binary metal oxide for high-performance supercapacitor and electro-catalyst for oxygen evolution reaction
Ceram. Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dinesh, M., et al. Mn–Ni binary metal oxide for high-performance supercapacitor and electro-catalyst for oxygen evolution reaction. Ceramics International, 2020. 46(18, Part A): p. 28006-28012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid room-temperature synthesis of nanocrystalline spinels as oxygen reduction and evolution electrocatalysts
Nat. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cheng, F., et al., Rapid Room-Temperature Synthesis of Nanocrystalline Spinels as Oxygen Reduction and Evolution Electrocatalysts. Nature chemistry, 2011. 3: p. 79-84.
